explicit_text: I was born in Vang Vieng, Laos in 1955. This is where my early life began before my family moved to Vientiane in 1979. Vang Vieng holds a special place in my heart as my hometown and the origin of my family's journey.

explicit_text: Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp in Saraburi, Thailand was our home from 1993 until 2004. It was there that several of my younger siblings and I were born and raised. The camp was a place of refuge and hope for a better life before we finally moved to the United States.

explicit_text: My family and I immigrated to the United States on September 16, 2004, settling in Saint Paul, Minnesota. This move was a major turning point, offering us hope for a better life after years in refugee camps. The United States became the place where my father carefully brought his qeej, symbolizing our culture and identity.

explicit_text: My father, Vang Thao, was born in Vang Vieng, Laos in 1955 and has been a central figure in our family's journey. He learned to play the qeej from a young age and carried this instrument with him through all our migrations. His dedication to preserving our culture through the qeej is something I deeply respect and want to embrace myself.

explicit_text: The qeej is a traditional Hmong reed instrument that my father has played since he was five years old. It holds great cultural significance, being played at funerals and the Hmong New Year. My father wrapped his qeej carefully and brought it with him all the way from the refugee camp to the United States, showing how much it means to him.

explicit_text: I am part of the Hmong people, an ethnic group with rich traditions and culture. The qeej is one of our important cultural instruments, and playing it brings respect within our community. Our people have faced many challenges, including migration from Laos to refugee camps and eventually to the United States, but we continue to honor our heritage.
